original_abstract: This research focuses on assessing the ability of large language models (LLMs) in representing geometries and their spatial relations. We utilize LLMs including GPT-2 and BERT to encode the well-known text (WKT) format of geometries and then feed their embeddings into classifiers and regressors to evaluate the effectiveness of the LLMs-generated embeddings for geometric attributes. The experiments demonstrate that while the LLMs-generated embeddings can preserve geometry types and capture some spatial relations (up to 73% accuracy), challenges remain in estimating numeric values and retrieving spatially related objects. This research highlights the need for improvement in terms of capturing the nuances and complexities of the underlying geospatial data and integrating domain knowledge to support various GeoAI applications using foundation models.
humanized_abstract: This paper investigates the capacity of large language models (LLMs) in understanding geometries and their spatial relationships. We employ LLMs like GPT-2 and BERT to train geometry classes, geometric attributes and spatial relationships in the well-known text (WKT) format of geometries. And we train classifiers and regressors based on the embeddings of LLMs-generated WKT for various geometric attributes. The experimental results show that the generated embeddings by LLMs have a good capability for retaining geometry types and understanding some spatial relationships, achieving an average accuracy of up to 73%; However, they have poor performance for predicting numerical attributes and retrieving spatial related objects. Further work is urgently needed for foundation models to learn the geometric attributes and spatial relationships in geospatial data in a more subtle way, and to incorporate domain knowledge for better realization of GeoAI applications.

The experimental results show that the generated embeddings by LLMs have a good capability for retaining geometry types and understanding some spatial relationships, achieving an average accuracy of up to 73%; However, they have poor performance for predicting numerical attributes and retrieving spatial related objects. Further work is urgently needed for foundation models to learn the geometric attributes and spatial relationships in geospatial data in a more subtle way, and to incorporate domain knowledge for better realization of GeoAI applications.
